data.title: SentinelOne Is Too Cheap To Ignore At 4x Sales, Near 20% Forward Growth
data.description:
SentinelOne (S) is facing two critical headwinds. First, the broader software sector is perceived to face generative AI headwinds, bringing sector-wide valuations to among the lowest that we’ve seen since the 2022 crash in tech stocks. Second, the company’s lack of profitability heightens the risk profile as investors may be growing wary of the length of the bull run. The optimist in me points to the still-robust forward top-line growth as well as the significant net cash on the balance sheet. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

S Stock Price

I last covered SentinelOne in August, where I upgraded the stock on what I believed was a rotation into software stocks. That ended up being terrible timing, as the software sector has seen a pummeling since then, with SentinelOne down 25% itself.

The poor performance has made the valuation too cheap to ignore.

SentinelOne Stock Key Metrics

SentinelOne is an enterprise software company in the cybersecurity sector led by its endpoint protection products. Even before the rise of generative AI, SentinelOne marketed itself as an AI-powered cybersecurity platform.

Historically, endpoint protection has been a fragmented market, with SentinelOne and CrowdStrike (CRWD) disrupting the legacy operators. Nowadays, CRWD has emerged as a clear winner in large part due to having a wider and deeper product portfolio.

That has not stopped the company from still posting strong growth rates. In the last quarter, the company generated 23% YoY revenue growth to $259 million, slightly exceeding guidance of $256 million. This was notably slower than the 22% growth rate posted by the larger CRWD. I suspect that underperformance has a lot to do with the struggling valuation.

The company made some progress in terms of profitability, with non-GAAP operating margins expanding 11.9 percentage points.

That said, the company did not make nearly as much progress in terms of reducing its GAAP operating loss.

The company ended the quarter with $870 million of cash and investments versus no debt.

Looking ahead, management guided the fourth quarter to see around 20% YoY revenue growth to $271 million. The full-year guide of $1.001 billion was notably below the high end of the prior guide of between $998 million and $1.002 billion. Given SentinelOne’s small size, investors typically look for beats to the high end of guidance, implying that management may be effectively reducing guidance targets.

On the conference call, management disputed competitive rumors, noting that they have “not seen any meaningful disruption from whatever competitor.” While the company has been quite acquisitive in the last year or two, management noted that there is “nothing impending” and that their platform is “incredibly competitive at this point.” Management also noted that they are at an “inflection point for sustainable quarterly free cash flow margin.” I suspect there are two ways to look at this. The optimistic spin is that the company might finally see more meaningful margin expansion. But the pessimistic perspective might wonder if management is implicitly signaling decelerating top-line growth ahead.

Is SentinelOne Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

In December, Citron Research highlighted a bullish view on the stock. That hasn’t appeared to help end the languishing stock price. SentinelOne appears to be facing the coinciding issues of broader weakness in the software sector as well as potentially entering a period of decelerating growth alongside lackluster profit margins. At recent prices, the stock traded at around 4x forward sales.

I note that net cash makes up over 15% of the market cap. In the analysis that follows, I do not subtract the cash from the market cap due to the company still not being profitable on a GAAP basis. I can see the company reaching 20% net margins over the long term. I apply a discount to the typical 30% that I assume for peers due to the company still being far from GAAP profitability. That places the current valuation at around 20x long-term earnings. I find consensus estimates to be potentially too optimistic given that they factor in some acceleration and minimal deceleration over the near to medium term.

Even if we assume around 15% to 17% forward top-line growth, the stock is trading at around a 1.5x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’), which looks rather conservative. The clear catalyst here is a more committed effort towards margin expansion, which I would expect to help bridge the gap between cybersecurity peers like Zscaler (ZS) and CrowdStrike, which trade at around 10x sales and as high as around 20x sales. I can see the stock eventually re-rating to around 6x sales, implying a valuation of around 30x long-term earnings (or 20x earnings if the company can bring margins up to the 30% range). That suggests ample multiple expansion potential to go along with robust top-line growth.

SentinelOne Stock Risks

SentinelOne is still far from GAAP profitability, increasing the execution risk. It is possible that management is unable to drive margin expansion as much as I expect over the long term. It is possible that it is getting disrupted by CRWD, with the implication of slowing top-line growth (as well as margin expansion). The lack of material profits means that the stock may face heightened volatility in a market downturn.

SentinelOne Stock Conclusion

SentinelOne may be facing great competitive and macro risks, leading to consensus estimates to look aggressive. Yet even assuming that growth falls to a disappointing 15% to 17% range, the stock looks cheap here. Investors appear to have taken a “risk off” approach to the software sector but may be overlooking the impact of the strong net cash balance sheet. SentinelOne has many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: Trinity Capital: Buying The Yield, Watching The Price
data.description:
Trinity Capital (TRIN) is a well-run, income-generating BDC with several quarters of reliable income-generating capabilities demonstrated (consistent early teens income on average). But the stock has also undergone a more than meaningful rerating in recent quarters too. The question, therefore, becomes whether price risk offsets the income credentials at current valuations.

I go through historical price charts, reconstruct historical Price to NAV data, and overlay that with management commentary and the current macro backdrop in this analysis. My analysis just about qualifies Trinity Capital as a Buy with an eye on several factors at current levels. This is clearly not the best time to deploy fresh capital, but fresh capital should still find positive returns ahead. The best play is to keep some cash ready to deploy over time as Trinity Capital navigates a unique macro setup.

Today's macro environment is not a VC boom period. Hence, Trinity Capital is not a VC beta play any longer, like it potentially was in 2020-21. It is now viewed as a cash-yielding lender in a regime where capital is relatively scarce. This means Trinity Capital gets to dictate structure and pricing (better collateral along with higher spreads ideally). But credit discipline is more important than ever too, with weak borrowers unlikely to find exit routes via rising valuations or IPOs.

NII durability is reasonably strong, but not bulletproof. Trinity’s loan book has reset at much higher yields over the last two years, and most loans carry rate floors. That means modest rate cuts are unlikely to cause a sudden drop in income. At the same time, dividend coverage remains thin, which tells you earnings are stable but do not offer a high degree of flexibility.

Overall, Trinity's upside from here appears capped by valuations and a stagnating NAV. Downsides could open up if credit quality slips (no signs yet). Which is why expectations on fresh capital deployment need to stay grounded.

Total Return Charts

The price charts show that Trinity Capital has not fully recovered from the 2022 pressures. Since Trinity Capital mostly derives total returns off distributions, any price reset is not necessarily recovered even over several quarters. As we will see, the share prices include a material rerating from 2024 with no material impact on share prices. This is classic income asset repricing at work, still not a long-term compounder.

The 3-year total return charts show how the total returns have been pushed up in the past year by this meaningful rerating phase, adding to the early teens yield. As of now, we are sitting near the peak of the total return cycle - that is, ~25% CAGR for investors who entered 3 years ago (compared to the ~12% CAGR levels seen earlier, mostly accounted for by dividends). And momentum is stalling. This indicates the easy re-rating phase is likely over, and correction risks open up more than further upside odds.

The Rerating Phase

To reconstruct the valuation trajectory, I collated quarterly NAV data (every 2 quarters) and used quarter-end share prices. The data clearly shows that Trinity Capital spent most of its time at deep discounts, initially trading at ~0.65x to NAV. This period of investor caution meant most of the total returns had to come from dividends alone. This includes the NAV peak periods like that seen in 2022 (where valuations still stayed heavily discounted). The bulk of the rerating that has driven high total returns actually happened since 2024. The current P/NAV ratio is now trading at a premium. If NAV does not expand materially from levels reported in Q3 2025, the current share prices imply prices at ~1.25x of NAV - which could extend the valuation normalization phase into clearly euphoric territory for Trinity Capital.

This rerating and migration to premium territory have been earned through time because of several supportive developments. Trinity Capital has navigated the worst of venture credit distress without defaults spiking to threatening levels. The NII coverage has been thin but consistent. On the flip side, while the narrative on credit quality and payouts has strengthened, there hasn't been sustained NAV expansion since 2022.

Dissecting Q3 results

While NAV expansion is stalled, it is looking stable. NII roughly offsets dividends. Realized losses exist but are not compounding. Unrealized swings reverse (not trending down).

The portfolio also shows that it is not positioned as a pure VC play, with ~15% of the mix in equipment finance (hard collateral). Rate sensitivity is positively asymmetric. According to Trinity Capital's estimates, downside from rate cuts is muted, while meaningful upside still exists for higher-for-longer rates. That is, NII does not collapse if rates normalize. Another positive that is supportive is the optionality from warrant positions that could add ~$0.2 upside per share in a conservative assumption of 2x multiples - not a core rerating trigger but supportive.

In Q3, Trinity Capital further raised $83m in equity at a ~19% premium to NAV. That should be accretive to NAV per share. 99% of investments were reported as performing at fair value. The company also reported its internal credit rating at 2.9 (out of 5), consistent with prior quarters. Non-accruals were only 1% of the total debt portfolio. The numbers do not point to any latent credit stress in the system.

There are some drags to further rerating, including aggressive share issuance that caps NAV per share growth potential. And the issuance is material - share count is up from ~54m to ~75m in almost a year, continuing dilutive trends seen since 2023-24.

Further, Trinity Capital is looking to lower leverage over time. This is great for de-risking, but not so good for RoE. In other words, Trinity Capital is settling into a conservative, income-led equity state where future earnings growth will primarily rely on fee income and volume, not leverage expansion.

Setting Expectations

The outlook for 2026 is all about defending the base, not expansion. I expect dividends to be stable and not much erosion in NAV/share if credit risks continue to be handled well. That is, the base outcome does not imply further rerating from here on - more about discipline around dividend funding.

The downside risks are theoretically stronger, though. Any structural erosion of dividend coverage (already thin) or NAV/share drop due to dilution, if not NAV stagnation, could pressure valuation premiums at play today. Which means new investors will need to keep an eye on higher non-accruals, slower originations, or spread compression.

For the upcoming Q4 results, I do not expect any shocks, but premiums will be tested even if a marginal NII decline shows up (given thin coverage). The other two areas to watch are credit quality and NAV/share stability. I feel the thesis will tilt to a stronger Buy only after at least a couple of quarters showing dividend coverage of above 110%. If accruals start crossing the 2% mark, the Buy thesis could be tested. I will also watch out for structural spread compression on new originations, particularly if they seem to be driven by competitive pressures rather than borrower quality.

Overall, despite some valuation compression risks outweighing odds of further rerating, I am comfortable issuing a Buy call for Trinity Capital because of its yield levels alone. The reason I call this a marginal Buy today is because the valuations are higher, and these are probably not the best levels to enter. However, a 13-14% yield looks maintainable, and that alone can absorb a P/NAV compression to ~0.95x over the next year and ~0.85x over the next two years. Fresh entries should set their expectations around mid-single digits, acknowledging the valuation risks and betting on yield stability. The 'failsafe' case is breakeven over two years and has the potential to still generate single-digit total returns over the long term - a bet that seems to have a path to green eventually. However, that assumes only valuations compress - dividends continue, and fundamentals do not change over the course of the next few quarters. Remain watchful and size positions that can allow averaging if Trinity Capital corrects.

data.title: TKMS: The European Marine Powerhouse To Buy
data.description:
With Europe looking to accelerate its push for security autonomy, European defense stocks may look attractive. However, we also note that given the nature of investments required to support demand each defense contractor stock should be assessed on a case-by-case basis. In this report, I am adding TKMS AG & Co KGaA aka TKMS (TKMSF) to my coverage. Since, this is my first time covering TKMS, I will discuss the company’s activities followed by a brief discussion of the FY25 results and provide a price target assessment.

TKMS Is A Leader In Conventional Submarines

If TKMS or thyssenkrupp Marine Systems did not pop up on your radar when looking for investment opportunities in the aerospace and defense industries, I cannot blame you. The company was spun off in October in October 20205 from thyssenkrupp with the company retaining majority ownership. Shareholders received 1 share of TKMS for each 20 shares of thyssenkrupp held. The spinoff gives the company access to capital markets with a focus on defense potentially providing investors with a better price for their shares.

TKMS is organized into three segments focusing on different product types:

  • Submarines
    • Type 212/212CD (Next Generation)
    • Type 214
      • Type 218SG (customized variant of the Type 214 for Singapore).
    • Type 209 (Next Generation)
    • Dolphin Class
  • Surface Combatants
    • Corvettes
    • Frigates
    • Patrol Vessels
  • Atlas Elektronik
    • Sensor & Sonar
    • Combat Management Systems
    • Underwater Weapons
    • Naval IT & Digital Systems

The company’s core competence is the design and manufacturing of conventional, meaning non-nuclear, submarines. From margin and strategic perspective, this is the company’s highest value business. Surface Combatants include the K130 class corvette and the F125 class frigates with strength in volumes while Atlas Elektronik provides high margin electronics, subsystems and software. With its three segments, the company is positioned as a vertically integrated aerospace and defense company with a focus on naval vessels and submersibles.

The Risks For TKMS: Competition, Platform Lock-In And Complexity

Currently investments in defense equipment, including naval vessels and submarines, is increasing. There are, however, risks. One risk is that each segment has their own set of competitors:

Platform

Competitors

Conventional Submarines

Naval Group, Saab, Navantia

Surface ships

Damen, Fincantieri, BAE

Electronics

Thales, Leonardo, Saab

However, we note that TKMS is a global leader in Conventional Submarines and a Tier 1 in Surface ships and Electronics. We note that TKMS has strength in all three segments compared to peers:

Company

Submarines

Surface Ships

Naval Electronics / Systems

TKMS

Global leader (conventional)

Strong

Strong (Atlas Elektronik)

Naval Group

Nuclear + strong conventional

Very strong

Medium (Thales-dependent)

Saab

Strong niche

Moderate

Very strong

BAE Systems

Nuclear only

Very strong

Strong

Fincantieri

Weak

Very strong

Weak (Leonardo-dependent)

Navantia

Emerging

Strong

Weak

Damen

None

Strong (patrol, corvettes, auxiliaries)

Weak–Medium (partner-dependent)

In some way, what Saab is missing is the scale that TKMS has. So, we see that that each company has their strength and weaknesses, but TKMS is an overall strong company in all three segments. Risks for the company include the competitive landscape with platform lock-in. Naval procurement is mostly a winner-takes-it-all set up and that means that once a navy selects a platform supplier, competitors are effectively locked out for decades. It provides some risk in bidding and selection process, but also provides a high degree of predictability including high margin services that are certain. It also means that absent of new program wins while existing programs are either peaking or sunsetting in terms of revenues there could be a lack in revenue growth or even revenue decline.

Another risk for companies like TKMS is that whether it are surface combatants or submarines, the products are complex to design and manufacture which means that there is a risk of program delays and cost overruns.

TKMS Backlog Surges To €18.2 Billion

TKMS revenues rose 9% to €2.2 billion with submarine sales rising 17% to €1.14 billion driven by production progress on major projections, a 12% decline on surface vessels to €503 million as multi-year projects are reaching the end of the manufacturing cycle and a 19% increase to €701 million on Atlas driven by a strong project pipeline. Gross profits increased 22% to €383 million with margins increasing from 15.8% to 17.6%. The submarines segment saw a 91% increase in gross profits to €139 million with margins lifted 4.8 points to 12.2% on a better margin mix. Surface vessels gross profits declined in line with revenues to €84 million with disciplined execution offsetting any margin headwind from loss of scale. Atlas Electronics saw its gross profits increase 10% to €159 million with margins declining 1.7 points driven by performance in Naval Weapon orders being weaker year-on-year.

The company grew free cash flow from €429 million to €784 million with EBIT increasing 53% to €131 million indicating margins of 6%. Order backlog grew 55% to €18.2 billion with €7.1 billion worth of orders for submarines, €1.2 billion in surface vessel orders and €685 million in orders for Atlas Electronics.

TKMS Stock Initiated With A Buy Rating On Naval Demand

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: The price targets are based on the listing in Germany (FWB: TKMS), which has higher trading liquidity than the OTC ticker TKMSF. This provides an improved ability to buy and sell shares at desired quantities and prices. Price targets and other financial metrics have been converted from EUR to USD.

For TKMS, I am initiating the stock with a Buy rating and a $140.18 price target providing 20% upside. We note that the stock is currently trading in line with expected earnings for 2026, which makes sense if we keep in mind that the market should be forward-looking. We also note that our price target is based on the company median EV/EBITDA, which is below the peer group valuation. We do note that ThyssenKrupp has retained a 51% stake in the spin-off. That is something to keep in mind for investors who are not interested in investing in majority-owned companies.

For TKMS, I am expecting 12.3% growth in sales annually, EBITDA growth of 19.4% annually but weaker free cash flows as the company is likely largely dependent on timing of orders and milestone payments causing fluctuations in expected free cash flow by year.

For TKMS, I am expecting EBITDA margins to grow as operating leverage improves. This should lead the company to grow margins from 6.7% in 2023 to 11% in 2027. As stated, cash flows have a high degree of variability and in that sense are not a strong metric to assess growth. For naval shipbuilders it is quite common that working capital requirements and milestone payments affect cash flows in a given year. In some way, you could see it as cash going out first and cash flows turn positive in later years. As a result, CapEx as a percentage of operating cash flow is also set to increase to around 45%-55%. The company is expected to pay a dividend and from its net cash position - there also is no reason to assume that the company wouldn’t be paying a dividend.

Conclusion: TKMS Is Looking Attractive

Spinning off TKMS from ThyssenKrupp in my view is a good move that allows the naval business to be valued as an aerospace and defense company. Even when we do not apply an EV/EBITDA in line with the peer group we see that the stock has upside. Given the increasing demand for surface combatants as well as conventional submarines supported by increasing defense budgets, I believe that TKMS provides an attractive investment opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Credo's Next Phase Begins
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While I see Credo (CRDO) as a fundamentally strong winner in interconnect, my approach is now clearly both tactical and fundamental. Since my last coverage, CRDO has been down 13% as the focus has clearly transitioned from discovery to pricing. The stock is trading off an aggressive curve of ~$1.19 billion revenue in FY26 (+173% from prior year) and has clearly assumed valuation compression rather than expansion.

The options activity is clearly indicating hedging rather than chasing the stock higher. The stock is trading in a clear uptrend from a technical perspective, though one that has clearly lost all urgency below the $145-148 level. My thesis is to stay constructive long term but disciplined near term, respecting valuation, positioning, and timing into earnings.

I Start With the Forward Revenue Curve, Because That’s What the Stock is Trading

When I look at Credo, the first thing that anchors my mind isn’t the last quarter’s revenue or margins. It’s the revenue path that institutions are expecting us to follow.

Street's model for the fiscal year ending April 2026 shows a projected revenue of $1.19 billion, or an astonishing 173.45% growth rate. That single piece of information tells me that the Street has already passed AI exposure and accepted Credo as a hyperscaler supplier. The curve then normalizes, yet remains a challenging one. Hence, the street expects $1.68 billion for April 2027, which implies a growth rate of 40.7% year-over-year.

The key thing that matters to me is not whether these numbers are achievable in isolation. It’s that the market is already embedding pauses, digestion, and re-acceleration into the model. This is not a naïve hockey stick projection. It’s a mature curve that assumes both strength and friction. That has implications in terms of how this stock behaves in the market.

Options Market Saying Hedge, Not Chase

The cleanest signal to earnings, in my view, comes from the derivatives market. While call open interest has been coming down substantially, put open interest has been rising. This does not happen because the market has suddenly turned bearish. This happens only when investors conclude that upside convexity is no longer cheap.

But what’s striking to me is where the positioning is. Call selling occurs at clusters of $165 to $200, and these numbers match up almost perfectly with the mean price target of $211.50, representing about 56.6% upside from where the stock is now. To me, this says institutions are realizing or limiting upside at levels where the consensus already believes the stock is fairly valued.

On the flipside, position the put clusters closer to the spot rather than far out-of-the-money. That's not a form of protection; that's a form of volatility insurance. The message is clear here: The market isn’t ready for a blowout. It’s ready for uncertainty.

Valuation Compression Doing the Heavy Lifting, Not Multiple Expansion

CRDO is presently trading at around 21.0x forward P/S for FY26, 14.95x for FY27, 11.65x for FY28, and 8.56x for FY29 before settling down to around 8.8x in FY30 and further compressing to around 7.4x. This tells me an important thing, that the bull case is no longer a function of multiple expansion. The market is pricing the value down as a function of revenue scale. That is, the entire bull case rests on Credo getting the curve, as opposed to getting above the curve.

Now, that’s a fundamentally different situation from the way things were set up in the prior stages of the cycle. On traditional measures, this is why the screen ranks poorly: the non-GAAP forward P/E is near 49.8x, the GAAP forward P/E is near 73.4x, the EV/sales forward is near 20.4x, while the EV/EBITDA forward is near 44x. These numbers appear to be quite high when compared to the sector medians

But that does not mean they're irrational. What it actually implies is that the valuation is explicitly forward-loaded. The stock is reflecting today's price on the basis of an expectation that the company is going to be a multi-billion-dollar revenue company with significant future relevance in AI interconnect.

A Technically Strong Stock That’s Lost Urgency

From a purely technical standpoint, Credo appears to be in good health. The primary uptrend remains intact, with no signs of panic selling, no violation of key trend support, and no evidence of forced liquidation

However, the momentum has subsided. The RSI has fallen back toward the middle. The volume has not confirmed the new high. Most notably, the stock has not been able to break back into the $145 to $148 price level, which has been a significant level of support.

That level is relevant to me as an area where past institutional demand may translate into overhead supply once that stock does consolidate. Unless CRDO breaks decisively above that level and holds that level, the stock's tape characteristics suggest that buyers remain content to wait rather than jump into the stock with conviction.

Meanwhile, the downside has been orderly. There has been no urgency to the declines. That's the hallmark of digestion: No chase on the way up, no panic on the way down.

How Do I Define Earnings Setup?

Prior to the earnings announcements, I have identified three scenarios in mind. If Credo beats expectations and increases investors's faith in forward momentum, the stock could certainly break through to the upside and cause those hedges to reverse. That's the bullish scenario, and I suspect it will require more than just a beat. It will require clarity.

But if Credo's results come out clean and largely similar to the curve, I believe the most probable scenario is continued consolidation. And that’s not bearish. That’s digestion. If earnings create uncertainty in the timing, margin picture, or customer spending rates, even if the longer-term picture remains the same, the stock can move down to re-reset its level without breaking its structural trend.

Takeaway

Credo is no longer a discovery trade; it’s now a precision trade. The stock’s price of $135 is now based on an aggressive forward curve rather than last quarter’s execution. The stock’s upside now depends on the company’s ability to meet expectations rather than beat them. The company’s business is good, but the stock’s margin for error is slim.

data.title: Net Lease Office Properties: The Endgame Approaches
data.description:
I've covered Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) three times since their spin-off from W.P. Carey (WPC) in November 2023. While the total return since my first piece has held up nicely against the indexes, I can't pretend I'm not disappointed with some recent sales. Most notably, NLOP just announced the sale of their KBR HQ for $66m, significantly below my estimate. Despite the impressive $40m for their Google building that closed at the same time, the stock has sold off as investors digested these prices. I think one final piece on NLOP is in order to break down the remaining range of outcomes.

Realized Sales

My initial pitch suggested 100% upside was possible on NLOP shares when they traded for $23. While 100% returns were quickly achieved by buyers of the initial spin at ~$10, the total return since my initial pitch has been about 25%. Most of the underperformance is a result of weak sales of European properties and poor realizations on the J.P. Morgan Dallas and PPD buildings relative to my initial expectations.

Lastly, there is KBR, which I initially estimated at $150m before lowering my value in subsequent pieces. This building was primarily valuable due to the >$20m a year lease with KBR for ~90% of the space, set to expire in 2030. As a result, it seemed appropriate to lower the estimate as rent was collected. However, the ~$45m of rent collected since the spin still only gets us a bit over $110m in value, about a $3/share hit to my initial valuation estimates.

It hasn't all been doom and gloom, as their CVS campus sold for over $15m ($1/share) more than my estimates, and Google exceeded my initial valuation by about $25m after paying off its mortgage. While Omnicom remains the last major property to sell, I expect it will exceed my initial estimate of $38m.

What's Left

There are 22 properties left for NLOP to dispose of, with about $26m of Annual Base Rent (ABR) and 4 years of Weighted Average Lease Term (WALT). I have assumed the sole remaining mortgaged property, Intuit, is worthless. My estimates for the remaining properties total $234m as follows:

As you can see, only four properties remain that can move our estimated liquidation value by $1/share. The Iowa, iHeart, and North American Lighting properties are all being valued fairly close to the remaining rent on the property, though I have applied some premium to what I expect is a fairly stable government client in Iowa (<$10m).

Omnicom is the last of the big properties, and I am assuming about an 8% cap rate. I think investors can get comfortable with this given:

  • Google just transacted at a similar cap rate, right down the road.
  • There have been three other creative campus comps in the area, for $500/sq foot, $500, and $400/sq foot. Note that they called out market rents of $55, well above the ~$33/sq foot Omnicom is paying, which should support extension/releasing efforts. Market rent of ~$6m on this property would suggest selling for $50m is still a double-digit cap rate.

For the remaining leftovers, Arcfield, Bankers, and APCO are all under contract for redevelopment. Arabella and Donnelly have been written down by the company, and I've adjusted my valuations down accordingly. Nothing else really moves the needle if I'm off by 20%.

Valuation

At $19/share, NLOP has a market cap of about $280m. After February's $6.75 dividend comes back to shareholders, the adjusted market cap will be about $180m, all else being equal. I estimate their year-end cash balance as follows:

$38.7m unrestricted cash at the end of Q3

  • +$13m October lease termination payment (Master Lock).
  • +$33m Thermo Fisher sale.
  • -$60.7m dividend.
  • +75.6m sale.
  • -$75.6m dividend.
  • +$15m cash from operations in Q4 (author estimate, similar to Q3-25).

Total = $39m unrestricted cash.

Therefore, my estimated EV after the latest sale is $141m, versus $234m of property value. I continue to expect ongoing overhead and commissions to be more than offset by property cash flow. I also don't see material remaining balance sheet obligations that won't travel with the remaining property sales. See the pro forma filed with the KBR sale as an example, where AP/AE liabilities exceeded the "Other Assets" that were transferred with the sale.

My estimate suggests $93m of NAV in excess of the EV, which is a bit over $6.50/share. We can round down and land at a $25/share price target for the remaining NLOP assets.

Risks

Remaining property sales disappoint for one reason or another

Interest rates spike, making it harder for prospective buyers to finance acquisitions.

NLOP takes a bulk discount on the remaining sales to expedite the conclusion of the process.

Tax treatment of dividends is not a return of capital, limiting upside in taxable accounts.

Conclusion

While NLOP has underperformed my initial hopes, they have delivered solid returns for investors as they systematically sell off properties from their portfolio. With four special dividends in the last few months, the liquidation is accelerating. I think there is about $6 a share of remaining upside for those who stick around to the bitter end, making it still worth the time for investors. This would bring the total return on the idea to ~50% from my initial coverage, not what I hoped, but that's why you buy with a margin of safety! Disagree with my estimates? Let me know why in the comments.

data.title: SHLD: Modern Warfare, Modern Returns
data.description:
The Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) is a paradigm change in the defense industry, which breaks through traditional heavy industry to focus on the high-margin realm of software-defined warfare. Its portfolio focuses on a niche where intelligence meets impact by identifying the foundational elements of contemporary national security: cybersecurity, robotics, and autonomous drones. In addition to the brains of warfare, such as advanced processors, sensor arrays, and data networking solutions, SHLD also allows investors to tap into the critical physical platforms, such as military aircraft, naval platforms, and advanced weapon systems. Rather than simply focusing on niche elements such as space launch platforms, defense-oriented fuel solutions, and technical simulation-based training solutions, SHLD allows investors to tap into the entire defense value chain.

The fund is entering 2026 on the back of a stellar performance in 2025 as the fund rose by an astonishing 73%. The performance was significantly robust compared to the broader market. However, the run has yet to end, as the fundamentals from several powerful catalysts will continue to support the fund. Total defense spending is on course to top $3.6 trillion by the end of the decade due to a massive rearmament cycle underway. Let's take a look at the fund, the catalysts, the various risks, the valuation, and the conclusion.

The Fund

Global X Defense Tech ETF has a main goal of investing in companies at the forefront of defense technology. The companies span from cybersecurity companies and advanced military systems builders to hardware companies operating in the space of robotics, fuel systems, and aircraft defense applications. The fund is set to replicate the performance of the Global X Defense Tech Index. You can read up in detail regarding the index on the link; I will cover some of the more important facts. The index consists of companies in three sub-themes: Cybersecurity, Defense Technology, and Advanced Military Systems. Only companies with revenues above 50% earned in these subthemes are included in the index. The index consists of 50 stocks. The criteria of the selection pool are relatively strict, in my opinion, and provide stakeholders with a generally higher-quality pool of companies. The companies must be listed in the developed or Emerging Markets range, excluding China, India, Kuwait, Pakistan, Russia, and Saudi Arabia. Market cap must be at least $200 million for new constituents and at least $160 million for already included companies. Average daily traded value in the last 6 months has to be a minimum of $2 million for pre-selection, must be traded for at least 90% of the trading days for the last 6 months, and free float must be at least 10% of all outstanding shares. The cap is set at 8% for a single company, and all companies with weights above 5% cannot exceed 40% of the index; all the other companies are capped at 4.5%. These criteria alone prohibit the fund from being overly concentrated.

We will rely on Seeking Alpha’s grading system to do an overview of the fund characteristics and compare it to peer funds. The fund has a Seeking Alpha C+ Expense Grade, with a 0.5% expense ratio to blame for the grade; however, I would say that the expense ratio is comparable to other thematic funds. The Bid/Ask % Spread is alone graded A-, with a 0.04% Bid/Ask % Spread. Regarding liquidity, the fund has a Seeking Alpha A Grade, with ample daily liquidity, an average of 1.19 million shares traded daily over the last three months, and the average volume being around $82 million. Assets under management are also at a substantial level, being around $7 billion. The dividend yield is 0.46%, while the dividend growth rate has been high over the last two years. This earns the fund a Dividend Grade C.

The sector allocation is Industrials-heavy, with around 87% of fund holdings. Technology comes next with around 13%.

The fund has diversified international exposure while having a dominating United States exposure with around 60% of fund assets. Germany, Britain, and South Korea come next with 8.3%, 6.5%, and 5.9%, respectively. We know that defense cannot be outsourced, and the exposure to companies of these economies provides the fund the potential to benefit from the ongoing rearmament of Europe and wider geopolitics.

Compared to peers in the industry, SHLD is lagging in categories such as Expenses and Dividends, behind peer funds State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) and iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA). I do not think this is a handicap for the fund, as the prime investment thesis behind it is that the companies are growth companies, poised to benefit from structural trends, and the fund should not be looked at as a dividend fund, and we also covered that the expense ratio is elevated, but not so much for a thematic fund. The fund is ranked best in the Momentum, Risk, and Liquidity criteria.

SHLD has been operating since the end of 2023, and we will do a comparison with peer funds having that in mind.

The fund has delivered fantastic returns, with around 208% cumulatively for the period, far higher than the second-best performer, XAR, with around 146%.

$3.6 Trillion Defense Supercycle Underway

The investment thesis supporting the Global X Defense Tech ETF, begins with the potential U.S. 2027 Department of Defense budget, which would mean an increase in spending by 50% to $1.5 trillion and signaling the most radical departure ever from traditional spending habits, with much of the allocation potentially going to initiatives such as Sovereign AI, and the Golden Dome, multi-layered missile shield which is expected to cost around $500 billion and would embroil potential contractors Lockheed Martin(LMT), RTX(RTX) and L3Harris Technologies(LHX), all constituents of the fund. Furthermore, these technologies are further tested in real-time in real-world situations such as Operation Absolute Resolve in Venezuela, furthering their proof of operational strength and proof of concept validity for autonomous drones and electronic warfare suites, potentially triggering new orders for constituents such as L3Harris.

As such, this operational success is supercharging a broader global rearmament cycle where projected global defense spending now is on track to hit $3.6 trillion by 2030-nearly 33% above 2024 levels. Central to this growth is the European defense supercycle, wherein NATO nations have confirmed new 3.5% of GDP spending targets, bolstered by the EU's SAFE (Security Action for Europe) fund. This underpins a resilient revenue floor for SHLD's European champions, including Rheinmetall and Thales, as they modernize regional defenses. Most importantly, though, this transition toward software-defined systems is fundamentally changing the sector's financial profile, as companies move away from hardware-heavy production, and margin profiles are set to expand from historical low double digits toward levels consistent with the technology sector.

Looking forward towards the coming decade, the fund stands out as the main beneficiary of the Agentic AI and JADC2 regime. The Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2) construct has an estimated market size of $56.9 billion by 2035, which essentially means creating a "network of networks," thereby enabling every sensor to communicate directly and instantly with every shooter. The JADC2 evolution has also been further spurred by Agentic AI, with the potential to augment as much as 36% of tasks performed in the industry, furthering the prospects of margin expansion. All in all, the wise exposure criteria of the fund have successfully shifted the fund to incline to higher-margin companies and encapsulated the global security landscape, which could be heading towards multi-decade growth, given geopolitical risks and the increasing polarization.

Valuation

After such a fantastic year for the fund, I expected to be met with adjusted, higher multiples, but quickly I realized that earnings far outpaced the price return and that P/E actually contracted, from an average 36.67x in 2025 to around 30x now.

Other multiples such as P/B and P/S are somewhat higher than the peer group, while long-term earnings are double-digit, above the average. Not a surprise here, given what we previously discussed.

The fund is in an upward trend, breaking above the 20-, 50-, and 200-day MA.

Risks

Although the structural pillars for the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD) appear quite strong, the thesis faces a tricky risk stack for 2026, particularly around a potential re-rating given a parabolic 2025 performance. In other words, the P/E of approximately 30x and the current RSI of around 40 could mean potential weakness, given a disappointing quarter for the defense tech group could create a space for the fund to re-rate downwards. Another risk, a peace treaty between Ukraine and Russia, could end in 2026, with now the first trilateral talks between the US, Russia, and Ukraine taking place. We know the sensitivity of the sector to such activities, as witnessed by the temporary 4.5% drop in the sector after the 28-point Ukraine Peace Plan was unveiled by the US administration in November of last year. Politics is also a huge factor and will play a very important role. For one, the current administration is at risk of losing their Congress support during the 2026 midterm elections; thus, the huge $1.5 trillion budget could possibly be blocked or reduced considerably. Finally, the high-tech chip and defense industry is heavily reliant on rare earth minerals from China, and this creates potential risks connected to supply chain dependencies given any further trade escalations.

Conclusion

SHLD represents a defense tech-focused fund, underweighting the heavy industry low-margin traditional companies, and exposing itself to tech giants of modern war. After a whopping 73% increase last year, catalysts remain, such as an increasing U.S. defense budget, which might amount to $1.5 trillion in 2027, and the world that's on track to spend $3.6 trillion by 2030. Not to mention real-world successes in Venezuela proving this tech is effective. However, we should be mindful of the warning signs or risks to the BUY thesis: the Ukraine war ending and slowing down orders, the 2026 midterm elections making a split Congress block the budget or heavily chop it, and the continued reliance on China for the minerals needed to build high-tech chips. It’s an adequate long-term bet on the high-tech era of warfare, in my opinion, but expect heightened volatility given the risks we discussed.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams: Why Some See Downward Potential
data.description:
I've long had Sherwin-Williams (SHW) in my "sights", so to speak. This is because the company is the peer of some of the European paint and coatings companies that I cover, such as AkzoNobel (AKZOY). I'm invested (heavily) in some of its peers, but I have no position in SHW. In this article, I'll detail why that is, and what could cause me to change my mind. Furthermore, I'll investigate how justified I believe the current outlook for the company is. By this I mean the outlook we have here on SA, where the last positive article for SHW was published in mid-2024, with all articles since then being either "HOLD", "SELL", or even "STRONG SELL". It's a relatively clear-cut picture that's being presented, and it's not one I necessarily see reflected across analyst markets outside of SA.

The average SA rating is a "SELL" - meanwhile, Wall Street has it at a "BUY", and Quant is in between - currently calling it a "HOLD".

By looking at the SA-provided factor grades, we can get an overview/idea of why the ratings are the way they are - they cite a very unfavorable valuation and growth metric (also momentum), while at the same time saying that Revision and profitability is "top notch" here, with profitability/margins being at "A".

So what's the truth, and what could cause this to change?

SHW is well-known in the US - less so in Europe, I believe. In the US, it's the market's largest provider of what is known as "architectural paints", selling across 5,000+ stores at mostly a higher price point than the competition. It has, as you can see in the article picture above, its own stores, but it also sells in other stores and big-box retailers. International exposure for the company does exist, but the company is mostly NA-focused.

Let's see what we have here.

Sherwin-Williams - What upside exists in US-focused paints and coatings

Over the past 5-8 years, the company has tried to edge into more traditional sales channels, while also keeping its store appeal across the US and the world. The M&A of Valspar some years ago enabled SHW to get in big with Lowe's (LOW). SHW is a bit of a contrarian company, because it keeps adding and opening up new stores. Part of this has to do with the business model. Paints and coatings are not necessarily products you want to ship long distances to the consumer, but rather buy on-site or close by. This is especially true for contractors and renovators. SHW has also improved for it's customers, by offering things like job site deliveries, while at the same time justifying (through its product quality and service), premium product pricing.

This is reflected in the company's sales profile - more than 60% of its products are sold not to DIYers, but to professionals.

This also represents a shift from the company's history - it used to be much more DIY focused. It also markets its consumer products differently, using different brands such as Menards.

One of the biggest arguments in favor of this company is how ubiquitous it is among professionals in the US. It's one of the most-loved brands for what it is and does. While the company does come with construction and renovation market correlation (which is volatile), there is something to be said for more rental property volume across the states, which is likely to drive sales and trends upwards in the future.

The now years-old partnership with Lowe's also is one of the primary arguments as to why this company could be likely to outperform even further.

SHW is BBB rated. It generates earnings of roughly $11-$12/share and year, and it pays a meager dividend of about 0.9% at today's valuation, but with a significant market cap close to $90B. This makes, on the perspective of yield, peers like AkzoNobel more interesting - the same is true for the initial-view P/E multiple, which currently stands above 30x P/E, with competitors trading at about half that, or thereabouts.

There's a significant premium baked into the share price here, it's also what is causing the neutral views and "SELL" ratings by many analysts. This is despite the fact that SHW has, what I would also agree with, a wide moat based on its current intangible assets. The network and brand power of its paints and coatings business is so durable and likely to outperform over time (in my view), that a significant premium for the company is justified. Following the acquisition of Valspar, the company is not just the biggest in the US, it's the largest paints and coatings manufacturer on the planet. It has a market share over 11%, which is very impressive given the size and sheer scope of the markets involved.

Closest competitors are AkzoNobel in Europe, PPG Industries (PPG) in the US, and maybe Masco (MAS), though Masco is comparatively small.

The business model for paints and coatings is attractive - and it has to do with how the work is done. Typically, unlike in most construction and renovation activities, material costs for paints and coats are minor. Unlike in things like metal and woodworking/reno, they make up less than 20% of the total material bill (with most professionals also getting discounts from manufacturers and stores). This is something I know because I have friends who work in a professional painting company, but there's support for this.

This means a few things.

First off, specialist labor costs are a big part of the overall project. This means that typically painters are more particular about their materials, and can afford to be, because their profit margins are higher. Also, choosing higher-quality materials means the job can last a lot longer. Additionally, painting is a very cost-effective way of renovating or increasing the value of a property. This means attractive market dynamics - and SHW, as one of the premier providers of products, is well-positioned here.

Recent results confirm strong trends despite a volatile market. Net sales were up over 3%, with 5% EPS growth - again, the paint stores segment outperformed, confirming the notion that these physical stores are really part of what continues to make the company attractive. Demand is very good - and this is what has the company trading at such significant multiples.

Valuation for Sherwin-Williams - Attractive only if you disregard 20 years of valuation trends

Sherwin-Williams is attractive only in a very narrow and premium scenario. If you look at where the company trades right now, close to $357/share, which represents a P/E of around 31x, this comes to a 60-100% premium to most of its closer peers, including AkzoNobel, which currently trades at about 15.3x. The latter-mentioned also comes with a 3.3%+ dividend yield, more than 3x the size of SHW, and a similar credit rating as well as lower debt. The things that SHW has going for it are better forecast accuracy and a more established and secured end market.

But let's say that you accept the company's premium of 29-30x, despite knowing how outsized this is to sector averages. Your expected returns, even if the company hits its own growth targets and earnings growth forecasts, would still not even meet 9% per year.

If you're an investor that values beating the market, I believe this is not all that favorable rate of return, and should be avoided in favor of better alternatives.

It should be mentioned that my PT of around $300/share or below is actually fairly high, in the context of both sector and analyst targets. I forecast a growth of 5% (above 3-4% of from others), with a 17-18% EBIT margin, above the 14-15% year longer-term averages. My assumptions include the forecast of generating a 35-45%+ ROIC on newly invested capital. This is to be put in context to the COVID-19 renovation surge (That's DIY, not pros), which might be distorting company numbers a bit, but we also have a slowdown in the overall housing market that's affecting things here. This slowdown has had the result that both pro segments and DIY segments remain somewhat muted, with upward potential (and that's also what I am, in part, accounting for when forecasting here).

The primary risks to the company are macro-related and valuation-related - and they make the company relatively unattractive when put next to something like AkzoNobel, which also has an upside on the 3-4 year forecast from the same trend upside SHW sees, but at a substantially lower forward forecast.

So, AkzoNobel offers potential market-beating performance at a forward P/E of less than 17x. SHW doesn't offer market-beating performance, but only half of that return, at a forward P/E of close to 30x. That's an imbalance, or difference that I find impossible to reconcile as of January 2026. For these reasons, and this alternative, AkzoNobel represents a "BUY" for me, while SHW is only a "HOLD". I wouldn't necessarily bet against SHW and go "SELL", but I would never buy at this price. The risk, if not for significant underperformance, for lackluster returns is so clear to me that I would be very careful here.

Risks & Considerations

Aside from general end-market cyclicality and volatile demand correlation (though again, there's plenty of proof to suggest that the company is atypically resilient when it comes to trends like this), the risks for SHW are relatively few. The company deserves, due to its moats and upside, a certain premium to its valuation and share price, and the history and current trends of its sales, including the latest quarterly results, suggest further potential upside for the company at the very least in sales.

However, the company trades at what I view to be an unacceptably high premium despite all this. No chemical or basic materials company (which is in the end what SHW is), should trade at this significant multiple. A market-beating upside can be gotten only in the case of significant further premiumization. Even in the case of high multiples as well as double-digit growth, you're still expecting only less than 9% per year, and that's including the yield.

For that reason, I consider the risk/reward profile to be unfavorable - and I believe you're taking too much of a risk in investing in the company here.

Thesis

Sherwin-Williams is the largest paints and coatings supplier in the entire world. I believe that at the right price, this company represents a must-have. The sector in which the company operates is very attractive, as evidenced by my significant stake in AkzoNobel and similar companies.

The company's valuation is currently prohibitive. I would give SHW a fair valuation of no more than $350/share, which puts the relative PT below $300 at the very least. This would require a more than 10-15% short term drop - and I believe the recent volatility and lack of investor returns (for most of 2025) reflect this unfavorable valuation.

For this reason, I give the company a rating of only "HOLD", and would look at alternatives for a better upside.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $300/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VPU: Time For Utilities To Turn Higher (Upgrade)
data.description:
Utilities have been a mercurial corner of the U.S. stock market. It has enjoyed periods of solid outperformance and risk-adjusted returns (often linked to the AI trade), while occasional stumbles in both absolute and relative strength have left income investors perplexed. At the moment, we are in one of those soft patches.

The Vanguard Utilities Index ETF (VPU) is in a 10% drawdown from its 52-week high. I had a "Hold" rating on the fund almost two years ago. Shares have returned 46% since then, outperforming the S&P 500. I am upgrading VPU to a "Buy" today. The valuation is fair, while the technical situation actually shows signs of potential.

VPU: Now Lagging SPY & VEU (Foreign Stocks) YoY

According to the issuer, "VPU seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of stocks in the Utilities sector using a passively managed, full-replication strategy when possible and a sampling strategy if regulatory constraints dictate. The fund includes stocks of companies that distribute electricity, water, or gas, or that operate as independent power producers."

VPU is a large ETF with $9.8 billion in assets under management as of Jan. 22, 2026. That’s up from $6 billion at the time of my February 2024 analysis. The annual expense ratio is low at just 9 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 2.71%--about double that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has turned weak, currently a lukewarm C ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The Risk Grade is also not the best, though that may be due to somewhat volatile performance during the 3-month decline. Overall, I would expect its beta to be below 1.

Finally, on liquidity, VPU is highly tradable, as evidenced by an average daily volume of more than 200,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just two basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar has high exposure to the value style, with more than half of the allocation on the left side of the style box. There is just 5% growth exposure, while 10% of the fund is classified by Morningstar as small-cap.

The 100% U.S. equity product’s valuation is not a bargain at 17.8x earnings, but VPU’s long-term earnings growth rate has ticked up by almost three turns since my early-2024 assessment. So, the PEG ratio improved.

VPU: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

VPU is a cap-weighted ETF, so it’s key to monitor both the fundamental and technical trends among its largest holdings. Nextera (NEE) is the biggest position at more than 11%, and it, along with many other large-cap Utilities-sector companies, reports Q4 results in the coming days. So, near-term volatility could be a bit higher than average.

I’d call out that VPU paid out a record-high cumulative dividend last year.

VPU: Holdings & Dividend Information

As for seasonal trends, January and February have often produced tepid returns. The first month of the year has averaged a small gain since 2016, while February has been negative. Bullish calendar numbers have been seen from March through May.

VPU: Neutral Near-Term Seasonals

The Technical Take

With a better growth-adjusted P/E and key earnings on tap, VPU’s technical situation is intriguing. Notice in the chart below that shares are at a critical technical juncture. The $178 to $181 range has been a pivot point historically. The risk/return profile is favorable, in my view.

What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls still control the primary trend. VPU is straddling that trend-indicator line right now, though the 50dma has acted as a near-term resistance mark.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has been in a bearish zone between 30 and 60 recently. Price-wise, VPU achieved its measured move upside price objective based on the depth of the late-2024 to April 2025 decline, and subsequent breakout.

So, the 10% correction makes sense. Now could be the time to enter the Utilities ETF or add to a position.

VPU: Shares Tag Long-Term Support, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on VPU. The PEG-ratio value is not all that expensive, while the technical situation appears to offer a long opportunity as we enter the heart of the Q4 earnings season.

data.title: 3 Monthly Dividend Payers I'm Buying Right Now
data.description:
As a well documented dividend growth investor, you may find it to be no surprise that I like and own monthly dividend paying investments. Right now there are three in particular that I am actively accumulating to grow my dividend income and retirement nest egg. What I like about these three positions is that they are diversified and provide income from multiple different sources: option writing, business development/financing, and commercial real estate.

Specifically, we are talking about the Neos S&P500 High Income ETF (SPYI), Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), and Agree Realty Corporation (ADC). Between these three, there are almost endless sources of income and each of them have been proven to provide consistent, growing returns for their shareholders. Here are what some of the major metrics look like over the past 3-year period.

SPYI MAIN ADC Portfolio Avg.
Avg. Yield 11.02% 7.51% 4.29% 7.61%
Avg. Dividend Growth 3.05% 5.28% 3.18% 3.84%
Avg. Revenue Growth N/A 5.92% 19.39% 12.66%
Total Performance 58.54% 108.78% 11.08% 59.47%

Based on this summary, if you were to have owned these three positions over the past three years, you would have observed an average yield of 7.61% with a dividend that is growing 3.84% per year while realizing total returns of 59.47%. I'd like to analyze these numbers a bit further to understand the context. First off, the total performance of the S&P 500 (SPY) over this same time period was 80.64% so from that high level view this portfolio has underperformed the wider market. However, there is more information that is needed such as investment goals and investor total portfolio composition before a true judgment can be made about these returns.

For myself, these are not my primary and dominant investments in my portfolio and they serve a purpose in the larger picture of my investment goals. More about my strategy can be found in the latest update from dividend growth portfolio here. The goal for these monthly dividend payers has multiple purposes, chiefly being a source of new capital to invest in other high growth positions without having to inject more capital into my account, and second to provide stability to my portfolio against stocks that are more sensitive to geopolitics and macroeconomics. In combination with all of my other positions, I find myself with a nice blend of total performance, yield, dividend growth, and stability.

Neos S&P500 High Income ETF (SPYI)

Option call writing can be a magnificent way to bring additional income to one's portfolio. I do this manually with positions in my accounts and enjoy the rewards of getting some "additional yield" in my portfolio. However, there are two important things that an option writing income ETF can provide, scale and ease. The first is the bigger of the two, being able to write options against AUM of $7.30B can provide a whole lot more income than my personal assets under management as they will have a plethora of different tickers they can write calls against in batches of 100 shares. The second benefit does come with a price of 0.68% in expense ratio, but relieves me of the burden to constantly analyze, write, and manage covered call options across my accounts.

The main risks associated with a covered call ETF like this are mainly reduced yields from the covered calls as premiums shift and price depreciation if the underlying stocks depreciate. For those fully relying on the income, these two risks can throw a wrench in financial planning. For me personally, this is less of a concern as I am not anywhere near retirement and using the cash purely for other investments or to continue snowballing.

Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN)

This is one of my favorite stocks in general, not just amongst monthly dividend payers. I was first exposed to BDCs and their small to medium business loans model when I was running a startup and it opened up a whole new world of finance to me that I was not as well-versed in. Main Street capital thrives in the area of financing smaller and younger businesses at favorable rates for either debt financing or equity. Companies like MAIN are the underlying back bone of the capitalistic economy that are the somewhat unsung heroes (I'm sure many would disagree with the term "hero") of the startup ecosystem and in-turn large scale capitalism.

Main Street provides a multitude of services from mezzanine financing, growth financing, small market funding, medium market funding, minority equity investment, majority equity investment, refinancing, management buyouts, and strategic acquisition funding just to name a few. Not only that but their investments are not concentrated in any particular region, per their latest earnings presentation, they are actually pretty evenly distributed across the country.

But what I really love about this company is the consistent, growing dividend. Unique as they already are with making monthly payments, they have also increased their dividend consistently without a single reduction in monthly distributions (and yes that includes the great recession and the Covid pandemic). The cherry on top is that they consistently reward shareholders with supplemental payouts throughout the year. Like clockwork for the past three years, MAIN has paid additional quarterly special dividends bringing the annual number of payouts to 16 per year!

Risks associated with Main Street are tied more closely to the broader market, as the economy thrives, MAIN thrives. I believe Main Street has demonstrated themselves to be reliable distributors through not one but two major economic crises that we have endured in the past 20 years, giving me confidence that their fortified balance sheet can weather any storm.

Agree Realty Corporation (ADC)

Agree Realty actually first caught my eye when living in Michigan and I would drive past their headquarters on my morning commute. Out of curiosity I began following their growth and have been impressed by what they have built and continue to build. ADC's business model centers around diversified investments in high-quality retail properties. While at first glance that may not seem so appealing, upon further research it is clear that Agree is the best of breed and has built a robust business model. As seen in their latest earnings report, ADC has set themselves apart from other retail net lease peers by achieving the highest level of investment grade tenants in the industry while also being the most diversified and high upside in rent per square foot moving forward.

While the risks are obvious for a commercial real estate REIT, I find comfort in the fact they just recently received an A- issue rating with a stable outlook from Fitch ratings as well as having a low net debt to recurring EBITDA multiple. These factors materialize in the form of 167 consecutive dividends paid with consistent annual dividend increases.

Summary

Every investment must be made with a goal and purpose in mind. While an averaged portfolio of these three have not provided S&P 500 beating returns, they have provided exceptional stability and nearly 6x the amount of dividend income annually. This makes each of these companies a great investment in my broader portfolio strategy and I will continue to build my positions in each. For those looking for reliable, high income every month these companies may be a great option for you as well.

I look forward to continuing my Seeking Alpha updates and analyses!

data.title: Fulton Financial's Strong Showing Justifies More Optimism
data.description:
The last couple of days now have been a pretty volatile time for shareholders of Fulton Financial Corporation (FULT). Shares of the company popped 2.6% on January 22nd. This was after management announced financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, and it followed a 4.3% increase in price the day before. However, as of late afternoon on February 23rd, the stock has taken a hit, clawing back some with a decline of 3.7%. This is disappointing to say the least, especially considering how solid the financial picture of the business was during this time.

Management exceeded estimates when it came to revenue and earnings per share. The company exhibited attractive growth during this time, not only when it came to the income statement but also when it came to the balance sheet as well. Asset quality remains elevated, and the stock is priced cheaper than other comparable firms for the most part. It is with this thought in mind that I don't have a problem maintaining it as a Buy candidate.

This follows my decision, back in early October of last year, to upgrade the stock from a Hold to a Buy. And so far, that call is proving to be successful. Shares are up 11.9%, comfortably outperforming the 2.8% increase for the S&P 500. Even after that nice rise, I would argue that further upside is likely. That is why I believe that maintaining it as a Buy makes sense right now.

The Picture Keeps Getting Better

Fundamentally speaking, pretty much everything is going right when it comes to Fulton Financial Corporation. Take deposits for the most recent quarter, which would be the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, they amounted to $26.59 billion. That's above the $26.14 billion the company had in the second quarter, and it represents a nice increase over the $26.13 billion it ended 2024 with.

What's really exciting here is that this came about at a time when brokered deposits barely rose. They increased from $843.9 million at the end of 2024 to $855 million at the end of 2025. These are high-cost deposits that come from other financial institutions and, as such, are generally not as reliable and attractive as traditional deposits are. This means that the vast majority of the expansion that the company saw came from organic deposit growth. And that's the best kind of growth.

When it comes to other aspects of the balance sheet, one area of growth that we saw involved loans. The company had $23.78 billion worth at the end of 2025. That's up a bit compared to the $23.67 billion the institution had at the end of the previous fiscal year. The greatest amount of loan exposure to the institution had here involved the $9.82 billion tied up in commercial mortgage real estate loans. Another $6.67 billion was tied up in residential mortgage real estate loans. And then we had commercial and industrial loans at $4.54 billion. No other category, besides the $1.24 billion of real estate home equity loans, exceeded the $1 billion mark. The smaller categories involved real estate construction loans, consumer loans, and other miscellaneous loans.

During 2025, the management team at Fulton Financial Corporation increased its exposure to securities also. These went from $4.81 billion in 2024 to $4.83 billion in 2025. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, dropped from $1.20 billion down to $1.18 billion. You might be perplexed about where a lot of the deposits went. The increases in securities and loans aren't enough to account for them, and we obviously didn't see an increase in cash.

The answer involves debt. Management made a concerted effort to right-size its balance sheet during the 2025 fiscal year. They decreased debt sharply from $1.78 billion to $1.30 billion. The goal here is to not only reduce leverage but to do so in a way that improves profit margins. And as you will see in a moment, that is exactly what transpired.

When it comes to the income statement, things are looking up as well. Management reported net interest income for the final quarter of 2025 of $263.1 million. That's up from $236.9 million reported in the previous year. A lot of this improvement came from a decline in the company's provision for credit losses from $16.7 million to $2.9 million. However, the increase in the size of its balance sheet, as well as the overall change in its composition, was also responsible.

Net interest margin improved from 3.41% to 3.59%. One thing that certainly helped here was that the cost of deposits dropped from 2.72% to 2.32%. As interest rates come down, banks are able to reduce how much they pay depositors immediately. By comparison, most assets see reductions in net interest expense more slowly, especially when it comes to fixed assets.

Non-interest income also performed well, rising from $65.9 million to $70 million. This primarily came from increases in cash management revenue, wealth management revenue, card revenue under the consumer banking category, and overdraft revenue under consumer banking. It is worth noting that when it comes to revenue as a whole, which is defined as net interest income before the provision for credit losses plus non-interest income, management reported $340.4 million. In addition to being above the $326.3 million reported the previous year, it also exceeded analysts’ estimates by $3.5 million. This helped to push net profits up, climbing sharply from $66.1 million in 2024 to $96.4 million in 2025. This meant a surge in earnings per share from $0.36 to $0.53, ultimately allowing the company to beat analysts’ estimates by $0.03.

Using the results the company achieved for 2025 as a whole, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced relative to earnings. Typically, I like to see banks trade at a price-to-earnings ratio of 10 or lower. And what we have here is a reading of 9.3, which clears that hurdle. It also makes the company the cheapest of the five institutions that I compared it to in that chart.

It's also pretty cheap relative to these firms when it comes to the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see that, on a price-to-book basis, only one of the five firms is cheaper than it is. And when it comes to the price-to-tangible book approach, it is the cheapest of the group.

We absolutely should not ignore asset quality in our evaluation of the business. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.23%. For context, I typically like to see a reading here of 1% or greater. So this is wonderful. It also happens to put the company slightly above the average, placing it above three of the five banks that I stacked it up against. The subsequent chart shows the same relative ranking, putting it above three of the five firms when it comes to return on equity. The reading of 11.69% here exceeds the 10% minimum that I look for.

Even though I am a fan of what I see here, I'm not going to say that I like everything about the institution. There is one area of improvement that needs to be achieved. And that involves credit quality. According to management, 0.58% of the company's assets were classified as non-performing during the quarter. This was admittedly down nicely from the 0.69% reported a year earlier. Meanwhile, we did see a drop in the percentage of loans that were non-performing from 0.92% to 0.76%. But I do prefer that both of these metrics be 0.50% or lower. The fact that we are trending lower, however, is wonderful. It's exactly the direction that we need.

With 2026 now well underway, one thing that should be mentioned is that management has their own thoughts about what the year has in store for the company. I am encouraged by the fact that they see mid-single-digit loan growth for this year. The company acknowledged that there are headwinds. But they believe that these are more moderate in nature. The company will counter these headwinds by hiring new bankers and by benefiting from momentum when it comes to loan obligations. For the year as a whole, the company is targeting net interest income of between $1.12 billion and $1.14 billion.

Using the midpoint of this and the midpoint of its loan loss provision estimate, we are looking at total net interest income of about $1.07 billion. This would be above the $1 billion reported for 2025. Non-interest income is being forecast at between $285 million and $300 million. This compares favorably to the $276.8 million that the company reported for 2025. And if we take the rest of management's guidance into consideration, we are likely looking at net profits of $388.8 million, which would be up from the $381.4 million reported the previous year and would translate to a forward price-to-earnings multiple of 9.1. So this is certainly good progress.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I am encouraged by what we see here. The market was right to push shares up after results came out and wrong, I believe, to pull them back down again. FULT stock is attractive from a fundamental standpoint and is attractively priced. Asset quality is high, and, even though credit quality metrics are worse than I'd prefer, they are trending better. When you add all of this together, I believe that this justifies a Buy rating.
